## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hello and welcome to lesson 10 of module 5 of the 10X Business Buying Accelerator. My name is Jake. I work with Grant and Brandon at the 10X Advisor Network and I'm going to walk you through the 12 month cash flow forecast tool included in the research section of this course. So a brief overview of this document. What it does is it helps you forecast the actual cash flow in the business month to month for the next 12 months. And this is actual cash in the bank account. This isn't revenue. So this is actual money in the bank account month to month. So you can see your actual operating cash flow. A quick note about how to use this tool. Any cell with a gray background needs to be updated by you as the user. Anything else will auto populate based on the inputs in those gray cells. So any of these white cells you do not want to adjust their pre populated formulas. If you do adjust them it might mess up the formula and then screw up some of the calculations down here. So so if you do mess with one of these fields and you can't fix it just go back to the research section and re download the tool and then you can start over. I'm going to walk through each cell in each section. This first one is just the business that you're acquiring. So in this case I'm acquiring the XYZ company. The next is the closing months. So this is the month that you're closing the deal by which the next 12 months will be populated here starting with the month following it and then ending in the month the same month one year out. So if I wasn't closing in March and I was closing in June I could change this to June 2022 and you'll see that these all update. We'll change it back to 20 March 2022 for now and you'll see these columns all update with the correct dates. The next section is starting cash. So the only cell you need to mess with here is the first cell for the first month following closing. So this number will basically be any cash that's currently in the business's bank account after closing. So all of these other ones will update based on the calculations and formulas going on in this column. You'll see that the starting cash for the following month is based on the end cash for this month and so on. So all of these numbers will auto populate. The only one you have to adjust is this first one here. So in this case we have $150,000 in the bank account day one after closing. The next section is cash inflow. In the cash inflow section we're looking at any cash coming into the business. So these are invoices that are actually being paid or any other sources of income.

## [03:02 - 06:05]

So you can see in our example there's $3 million a year in invoices being paid and it's not spread out evenly. So in the case where you have a business that is seasonal and you're getting more business in certain months compared to others, in this case we have a lol in the summer period and then cash flow picks back up near the end of the year and beginning of the following year. So you can see how you can kind of work in the seasonality there. You can base this off of previous year data and knowing the industry that the business you're acquiring is in. You can kind of gauge and forecast based on the season and the amount from previous years. This other column is any other additional income. For example, maybe you own the building that you work out of but you have additional space and you've rent that space out to another business. In this case for $2,500 a month. And then it'll tally up the total amount. So your total inflow month to month. That's just these two numbers added together and you can kind of see how it all stacks up. The next section is cash outflow. So obviously cash outflow is any money leaving the business, any actual cash leaving the business, not revenue again. So we have four sections here, invoices paid. So this is any cash leaving the business to maybe suppliers or merchants each month. And you can see that this also is seasonal too based on the invoices coming in. So our costs diminish when we're not doing as much business. And then they pick back up as we pick up business as well. So you can kind of factor that into your forecasting model based on the business that you're acquiring. The next is office rental. So this is any space you're renting to conduct business in. Maybe this is a storefront. This is an office space or any other facilities that you're working out of. Employee salaries, this is how much money you're paying employees every single month. And then if you were doing bonuses annually or quarterly or whatever you're doing, you want to adjust your forecast to include that. Obviously this number and when you pay them is dependent on you as the business owner. But in this case, we're paying out bonuses in December at the end of the year. So that's why this number is higher in December. Next is other expenses. So this is any other cost of doing business, whatever cost every single month to continue operations that wasn't included above. And then you'll see a nice tally. The total outflow is just all of these numbers combined. And then you'll see your operating cash flow. So this is your total inflow, all the money coming into the business minus your outflow, all the money leaving your business. And you can kind of gauge what your cash flow is going to look like month to month. You can see in this case, since we're seasonal, you can see that we have some trouble spots or red flags here in June, July and August, some negative operating cash flow and then

## [06:05 - 09:06]

it picks up at the end of the year. So maybe this is a weak spot that we can tackle when we take over the business, something to look at or optimize. The final section is the other cash flow section. So in this section, this is any other cash flow occurring in the business. I'll briefly cover each of the sections here. So interest paid is any interest on any debt in the business when you required it or any debt financing interest from any of the financing options you might have done to acquire the business. Interest received, this could be money in the bank account, the business's bank account occurring interest month to month. Taxes paid, traditionally you're going to be paying taxes quarterly and then once per year you're paying a large corporation tax usually around September. Capital expenditure, so this could be money that you're spending to acquire new equipment or machinery. For example, let's say you were picking up a contract in May that required some new equipment. You purchased new equipment to do that and then in August after you've completed the contract you are selling back some of that equipment or machinery and then maybe later in a year you're noticing redundancies in your machinery or equipment or things that you're not using or don't need anymore. You can sell them and gain cash flow that way. Investment received, so in this example we don't have any additional investments but this would be any additional investments of money being injected into the business of actual cash hitting the bank account. This could be selling equity in the business so you receive cash that way and angel investor investing money. So let's say in January we expected cash flow in the business from an additional investment of $200,000 so we can add that in and you can see how it adjusts the numbers. This is investment repaid so in this example we're paying $5,000 a month repaying an investment. Maybe this is seller financing where month to month we're paying them money based on that seller financing deal and then at the end of the first year we're giving them that large balloon payment of $200,000 and maybe your deal isn't set up this way. Maybe the deal is set up where it's just split up month to month. So you could split up this $200,000 every single month and pay it monthly but in this example we're paying $5,000 a month to repay investments and then in the case of seller financing we're paying the seller $200,000 at the end of year one. And now we see the totals here so this is other cash flow totals. This is just the column added all together you can see here and then we have our net

## [09:06 - 10:51]

cash flow. This is our operating cash flow minus that additional cash flow total and this net cash flow item is the operating cash flow plus whatever other cash flow totals there are. You can see the formula here and then we have two final rows here so this is your end cash so at the end of the month after we've taken the starting cash and we've forecasted what money we're expecting in and what money we're expecting out we get a total end cash so about almost $40,000 of additional cash added into that starting cash balance so we're going to end with almost $190,000 and then as we talked about earlier this number is now getting fed into the next month starting cash and then we rework the forecasting again for this month and it just recycles going all the way through. So you can kind of get a gauge of where your end cash is going to be month to month and then cash build is just showing at the end of one year what is the difference between the cash in the business now and the cash in the business when you took over. So this is the 12 month cash flow forecast again this is based on actual cash coming in and out of the business so this will show you your actual operating cash flow how much money you'll have leaving or coming into the bank account every single month. You can kind of get a gauge of where your breathing room is where your weak spots are if you have any troubles or red flags that you need to think about so that covers everything in lesson 10 on the cash flow forecasting model. Brandon will see you in the next lesson where he's covering deal structures.
